December 2, 1992
Fair Political Practices Commission
Irgal Division
428 J Street - Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
To Whom It May Concern:
I have recently been elected as a Council Member with the City of Grover Beach.
Currently I have a dental business in the City and provide services to many City
employees, residents, and business owners in the City. After reviewing the political
Reform Act with City statr, I felt it necessary to seek advice and clarification ftom the
FPPC concerning my business arangEents.
It is my understanding that income from my dental business would be exempt from the
$250 income limit for conllict under Section 87103.5 of the Political Reform Act.
Specifically, I understand that my dental business woutd quali! as a "service,, under this
section.
I would also like to seek clarification from you concerning ttre use of surplus campaign
funds. Under Section 89519 of the Political Reform Ac! I understand that I may use
surplus campaign funds for civic organizations. specificalty, I would like to use the funds
1o p_ay for new street signs for the city to reflect the recent voter-approved name change.
In ttris case, the contribution of surplus funds would be paid to the city for subsequent
purchase of the new sigrs.
Tq* ,o, for your assistance in responding to my concerns. I am eager to obtain your
advice, as I just assumed my seat of ofEce on December l, lgg2.
Sincerely,
E *r.! / A^rr[r* a,,o )
RONALD P. ARNOLDSEN, DDS
COUNCIL MEMBER

sections.question: It is my understanding that income from my dental business would be exempt from the $250 income limit for conflict under Section 87103.5 of the Political Reform Act. Specifically, I understand that my dental business would qualify as a "service" under this section. I would also like to seek clarification from you concerning the use of surplus campaign funds. Under Section 89519 of the Political Reform Act, I understand that I may use surplus campaign funds for civic organizations. Specifically, I would like to use the funds to pay for new street signs for the city to reflect the recent voter-approved name change. In this case, the contribution of surplus funds would be paid to the city for subsequent purchase of the new signs.
sections.question_synthetic: A newly elected council member seeks clarification on whether income from his dental business is exempt from the $250 income limit under Section 87103.5, and whether he can use surplus campaign funds to pay the city for new street signs under Section 89519.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a council member to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice sections present. Document contains clear questions regarding Political Reform Act sections 87103.5 and 89519. Some OCR errors present but overall document is legible.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: It is my understanding that income from my dental business would be exempt from the $250 income limit for conflict under Section 87103.5 of the Political Reform Act. Specifically, I understand that my dental business would qualify as a "service" under this section. I would also like to seek clarification from you concerning the use of surplus campaign funds. Under Section 89519 of the Political Reform Act, I understand that I may use surplus campaign funds for civic organizations. Specifically, I would like to use the funds to pay for new street signs for the city to reflect the recent voter-approved name change. In this case, the contribution of surplus funds would be paid to the city for subsequent purchase of the new signs.

embedding.summary: Newly elected Council Member Ronald P. Arnoldsen requests FPPC advice on two matters: whether his dental business income qualifies for exemption from the $250 income limit under Section 87103.5, and whether surplus campaign funds can be used to pay for city street signs under Section 89519.
